content: Ruler/pixel-to-real-world calibration pitfalls: perspective distortion occurs when the measurement plane (ruler) is not parallel to the camera sensor plane, producing a pixel-to-metric scale factor that is spatially non-uniform across the frame rather than a single constant — using one scalar px/cm ratio derived from a tilted ruler silently biases every measurement in that shoot. Because scale factors are only valid 'throughout the field of view' when distortion-corrected, and distortion-correction targets (e.g., checkerboards) need to be calibrated per camera position, any change in camera distance/angle between shoots invalidates a previously-derived conversion factor — this is the formal justification for the project's per-session (not global) calibration factor. RMS calibration errors in comparable machine-vision setups run ~0.02-0.7mm depending on target size, i.e. calibration error is real but typically small relative to gross setup mistakes (tilt, wrong distance) if the ruler is reasonably flat and parallel.
